content:
# Acts Chapter 26 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 257-258 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_026_page_001.jpg, ACT_026_page_002.jpg

---

**[1]** Then Agrippa said to Paul, "You have permission to speak for yourself." So Paul raised his hand and began his defense:

**[2]** "I consider myself fortunate, King Agrippa, that it is before you I am making my defense today against all the charges brought by the Jewish leaders,

**[3]** especially because you are well acquainted with all the customs and controversies among the Jews. So I ask you to listen to me patiently.

**[4]** "The way I have lived from my youth — how I spent my earliest days among my own people and in Jerusalem — is well known to all the Jews.

**[5]** They have known me for a long time and can testify, if they are willing, that I lived as a Pharisee, following the strictest party of our religion.

**[6]** And now I stand on trial because of my hope in the promise God made to our ancestors —

**[7]** the very promise our twelve tribes are straining toward as they worship God earnestly, day and night. It is for this hope, Your Majesty, that I am being accused by the Jews.

**[8]** Why should any of you consider it unbelievable that God raises the dead?

**[9]** "I myself was once convinced that I should do everything possible to oppose the name of Jesus of Nazareth.

**[10]** And that is exactly what I did in Jerusalem. Armed with authority from the chief priests, I threw many of God's people into prison, and when they were sentenced to death, I cast my vote against them.

**[11]** I had them punished again and again in every synagogue, trying to force them to blaspheme. So obsessed was I with hunting them down that I pursued them even to foreign cities.

**[12]** "It was on one such mission that I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,

**[13]** when at midday, Your Majesty, I saw a light from heaven — brighter than the sun — blazing around me and my companions on the road.

**[14]** We all fell to the ground, and I heard a voice speaking to me in the Hebrew language: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? You are only hurting yourself by fighting against the goad.'

**[15]** 'Who are you, Lord?' I asked. And the Lord said, 'I am Jesus — the one you are persecuting.

**[16]** But get up. Stand on your feet. I have appeared to you to appoint you as my servant and witness — both of what you have already seen and of what I will yet reveal to you.

**[17]** I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am now sending you

**[18]** to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light, and from the dominion of Satan to God, and receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are made holy through faith in me.'

**[19]** "So then, King Agrippa, I did not disobey this heavenly vision.

**[20]** Instead, I declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout all of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should turn from their ways to God and prove their change of heart by how they live.

**[21]** This is the reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.

**[22]** But God has been my help to this very day, and so I stand here testifying to both the powerful and the humble. I say nothing beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen:

**[23]** that the Messiah would suffer, that he would be the first to rise from the dead, and that he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles."

**[24]** As Paul was making this defense, Festus shouted, "You are out of your mind, Paul! Your great learning is driving you insane!"

**[25]** But Paul replied, "I am not insane, most excellent Festus. What I am saying is the sober truth.

**[26]** The king understands these things, and I can speak freely to him. I am convinced that none of this has escaped his notice, because it was not done in some hidden corner.

**[27]** King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do."

**[28]** And Agrippa said to Paul, "In so short a time, are you trying to make me a Christian?"

**[29]** Paul replied, "Whether it takes a short time or a long time, I pray to God that not only you but everyone listening today would become what I am — except for these chains."

**[30]** Then the king rose, along with the governor and Bernice and those sitting with them.

**[31]** As they withdrew, they said to one another, "This man has done nothing deserving death or imprisonment."

**[32]** And Agrippa said to Festus, "This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 5 — "the strictest party of our religion":** The Greek (akribestatēn hairesin) refers to the Pharisees as the most exacting sect. I rendered "strictest party" to convey the rigor without the modern negative baggage of "sect."

**Verse 7 — "straining toward":** The Greek (elpizei katantēsai) carries urgency — the twelve tribes are not passively waiting but actively hoping to arrive at this promise. "Straining toward" captures the forward lean of that expectation.

**Verse 8 — "Why should any of you consider it unbelievable":** Rendered as a direct, pointed rhetorical question. The Greek (ti apiston krinetai par' hymin) is confrontational — Paul is challenging the room, not musing philosophically.

**Verse 11 — "So obsessed was I":** The Greek (perissōs emmainomenos) conveys an excessive, raging fury. "So obsessed" captures the compulsive, unrelenting nature of Paul's former persecution without resorting to clinical language.

**Verse 14 — "You are only hurting yourself by fighting against the goad":** This is a well-known Greek proverb (sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein) — an ox kicking against the pointed stick only drives it deeper. I kept the image of the goad rather than fully modernizing, because it is vivid and comprehensible, but added "you are only hurting yourself" to make the force of the proverb explicit.

**Verse 18 — "from the dominion of Satan to God":** The Greek (exousias tou Satana) is literally "the authority/power of Satan." "Dominion" conveys the territorial, ruling sense of exousia better than the flatter "power."

**Verse 20 — "prove their change of heart by how they live":** The Greek (axia tēs metanoias erga prassontas) literally reads "doing works worthy of repentance." I restructured to make the logic transparent: genuine turning is demonstrated by changed behavior.

**Verse 28 — "In so short a time, are you trying to make me a Christian?":** The meaning of Agrippa's reply (en oligō me peitheis Christianon poiēsai/genesthai) has been debated for centuries. The Sinaiticus reading supports the sense of Agrippa deflecting with a mix of incredulity and dry humor. I rendered it to preserve that tone — not quite dismissive, not quite sincere — because the ambiguity is the point.

**Verse 29 — "except for these chains":** Paul's dramatic flourish here (parektos tōn desmōn toutōn) is theatrical and deeply human. He gestures at his shackles while wishing everyone in the room could share what he has — minus the imprisonment. The English preserves that poignant, almost wry moment.

— PNEUMA
